title: Technological innovation
description: Practice your skills in this exercise on technological innovation in the Gilded Age.

assessment_items.question_content:
Examine the table and answer the question below. 

|| **Rail travel time, 1870-1900** ||
Railroad company| Route | Mileage | 1870 Elapsed time (hrs) | 1870 Speed (mph) | 1900 Elapsed time (hrs) | 1900 Speed (mph)
- | - | - | -  | - | - | - 
Wabash | Chicago to St. Louis | 286 | 13:40 | 20.7 | 7:51  | 36.4
NY Central | New York to Chicago | 961 | 38:30  | 25.0 | 16:00 | 38.4

-Source: Robert J. Gordon, economist, *The Rise and Fall of American Growth: The U.S. Standard of Living since the Civil War,* 2016.

**Which of the following factors contributed most to the major trend depicted in the table?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: improvements in railroad technology
discovery of new cost-saving minerals
passage of immigration restrictions
creation of government subsidies

assessment_items.question_content:
Examine the table and answer the question below. 

|| **Rail travel time, 1870-1900** ||
Railroad company| Route | Mileage | 1870 Elapsed time (hrs) | 1870 Speed (mph) | 1900 Elapsed time (hrs) | 1900 Speed (mph)
- | - | - | -  | - | - | - 
Wabash | Chicago to St. Louis | 286 | 13:40 | 20.7 | 7:51  | 36.4
NY Central | New York to Chicago | 961 | 38:30  | 25.0 | 16:00 | 38.4

-Source: Robert J. Gordon, economist, *The Rise and Fall of American Growth: The U.S. Standard of Living since the Civil War,* 2016.

**Which of the following was a direct effect of the trend in railroad lines after 1870 shown in the table?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: new markets opened as perishable goods could travel greater distances faster
production of goods decreased because trains traveled shorter distances
cost of transport decreased because people could send goods farther
regional cultures disappeared as the country became more connected

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the excerpt and answer the question below. 

>“What has not the steam engine done for the Nineteenth Century? It speeds the locomotive across the continent . . . [and] the mighty steamship on the sea; it grinds our grain; it weaves our cloth; it prints our books; it forges our steel, and in every department of life it is ubiquitous, tireless, potent agency of civilization.” 

>-Edward W. Byrn, *The Progress of Invention in the Nineteenth Century,* 1900

**The author’s remarks in the excerpt most directly reflected which of the following developments during the late nineteenth century?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: improvements in power generation
development of interchangeable parts
advances in the steel-making process
enhancements in mass production

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the excerpt and answer the question below. 

>“What has not the steam engine done for the Nineteenth Century? It speeds the locomotive across the continent . . . [and] the mighty steamship on the sea; it grinds our grain; it weaves our cloth; it prints our books; it forges our steel, and in every department of life it is ubiquitous, tireless, potent agency of civilization.” 

>-Edward W. Byrn, *The Progress of Invention in the Nineteenth Century,* 1900

**The development discussed in the excerpt led to which of the following economic changes?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: It improved the processes used to produce goods.
It increased the cost of railroad transportation.
It expanded the labor force to include women.
It increased wages for the working class.

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the excerpt and answer the question below. 

> “New machines transformed both woodworking and metal working, to say nothing of whole new generations of machine tools. The ancient art of spinning and weaving gave way to giant factories in which a handful of employees tended machines that could produce more in a day than could have been made in months by hand. Shoes and boots also benefited from new machines that boosted production and lowered prices. Power machinery also made possible affordable carpets, furniture, and other fixtures for homes. . . New or improved machines dramatically increased production in many areas.”

>-Source: Maury Klein, *The Genesis of Industrial America, 1870-1920,* 2007

**The excerpt best illustrates which of the following developments?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the effects of technological innovations on the mass production of goods
the support from the federal government to improve the economy in the late 1800s
the replacement of jobs typically occupied by women with labor-saving machines
the rise in the cost of production following the introduction of new machinery

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the excerpt and answer the question below. 

> “New machines transformed both woodworking and metal working, to say nothing of whole new generations of machine tools. The ancient art of spinning and weaving gave way to giant factories in which a handful of employees tended machines that could produce more in a day than could have been made in months by hand. Shoes and boots also benefited from new machines that boosted production and lowered prices. Power machinery also made possible affordable carpets, furniture, and other fixtures for homes. . . New or improved machines dramatically increased production in many areas.”

>-Source: Maury Klein, *The Genesis of Industrial America, 1870-1920,* 2007

**Which of the following earlier trends was most similar to the pattern described in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the increase in innovations that improved the efficiency of production methods in the early 1800s
the development of the Trans-Atlantic trade system from the 1600s to the early 1700s
the enactment of tariffs and embargoes to support the sale of domestic goods in the early 1800s
the spread of a consumer revolution from Great Britain to the colonies in the late 1600s to the 1700s

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the excerpt and answer the question below. 

> “Soap production for the commercial market had started as a by-product of the meat-packing industry, with small companies processing animal fats for regional markets. In the late 1870s mechanical improvements in the mixing and crushing process used in making bar soap greatly expanded output. . . . By using the new machinery, Procter & Gamble was soon making 200,000 cakes of Ivory soap a day.”

>-Source: William Lazonick, *American Corporate Economy: Critical Perspectives on Business and Management,* Volume 4, 2002

**The developments described in the excerpt most directly reflect which of the following changes in the nineteenth century?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: improvements in production methods to increase efficiency
implementation of tariffs to promote domestic goods
expansion of the labor force to include young children
creation of the transcontinental railroad to open new markets

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the excerpt and answer the question below. 

> “Soap production for the commercial market had started as a by-product of the meat-packing industry, with small companies processing animal fats for regional markets. In the late 1870s mechanical improvements in the mixing and crushing process used in making bar soap greatly expanded output. . . . By using the new machinery, Procter & Gamble was soon making 200,000 cakes of Ivory soap a day.”

>-Source: William Lazonick, *American Corporate Economy: Critical Perspectives on Business and Management,* Volume 4, 2002

**According to the passage, which of the following best explains the most important effect that mechanical improvements had on the production process?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: It led to the production of large quantities of goods.
It decreased the average cost of factory-produced goods.
It increased trade with international markets.
It improved the standard of living for most Americans.

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the excerpt and answer the question below. 

> “[In] the hills and valleys round about, nature has stored those substances away with unequalled profusion. Besides placing a thick layer of excellent bituminous coal halfway up those winding heights, besides accumulating within them exhaustless supplies of iron, besides sinking under them unfathomable wells of oil and salt water, nature has coiled about their bases a system of navigable streams, all of which form themselves into two rivers, — the Allegheny and Monongahela, — and at Pittsburgh unite to form the Ohio, and give the city access to every port on earth. It is chiefly at Pittsburgh that the products of the Pennsylvania hills and mountains are converted into wealth and distributed over the world.”

>-Source: James Parton, “Pittsburgh,” *Atlantic Monthly,* 1868

**The excerpt is best understood as a response to which of the following historical developments?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the discovery of new natural resources to aid in the production process
the colonization of countries with valuable raw materials for manufacturing
the migration of thousands of Americans to valuable land in the West
the rise of Romanticism and appreciation of nature in the United States

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the excerpt and answer the question below. 

> “[In] the hills and valleys round about, nature has stored those substances away with unequalled profusion. Besides placing a thick layer of excellent bituminous coal halfway up those winding heights, besides accumulating within them exhaustless supplies of iron, besides sinking under them unfathomable wells of oil and salt water, nature has coiled about their bases a system of navigable streams, all of which form themselves into two rivers, — the Allegheny and Monongahela, — and at Pittsburgh unite to form the Ohio, and give the city access to every port on earth. It is chiefly at Pittsburgh that the products of the Pennsylvania hills and mountains are converted into wealth and distributed over the world.”

>-Source: James Parton, “Pittsburgh,” *Atlantic Monthly,* 1868

**A historian would most likely use this passage to illustrate which of the following aspects of the Gilded Age?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: how discoveries of and access to natural resources supported manufacturing
how Americans justified imperialism for greater access to raw materials
how manufacturing practices negatively affected the environment
how industrialization spread throughout the United States

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the excerpt and answer the question below. 

> “Iron replaced wood; steel replaced iron; and electricity . . . replaced horsepower. In 1870 agricultural production surpassed industrial production by about \$500 million. Both were increasing year by year. But by 1900 manufacturing had increased by more than four times. . . . 

> “Industrial growth and westward expansion were assured by the revolution in transportation and the revolution in communications. . . . A transcontinental railroad network brought farm and factory, country and town closer together. Telegraph and telephone, electricity and press increased public knowledge, business efficiency, and political debates.” 

>-Source: Sean Dennis Cashman, *America in the Gilded Age,* 1993

**According to the passage, which of the following best explains the most important effect that innovations like the telegraph had on the American economy?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: It improved the organization of the production process.
It opened the United States up to foreign trade partners.
It developed a higher standard of living for most Americans.
It facilitated the concentration of wealth to business leaders.

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the excerpt and answer the question below. 

> “Iron replaced wood; steel replaced iron; and electricity . . . replaced horsepower. In 1870 agricultural production surpassed industrial production by about \$500 million. Both were increasing year by year. But by 1900 manufacturing had increased by more than four times. . . . 

> “Industrial growth and westward expansion were assured by the revolution in transportation and the revolution in communications. . . . A transcontinental railroad network brought farm and factory, country and town closer together. Telegraph and telephone, electricity and press increased public knowledge, business efficiency, and political debates.” 

>-Source: Sean Dennis Cashman, *America in the Gilded Age,* 1993

**Which of the following earlier developments had an effect most similar to that described in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the innovation in steam power in the 1820s
the creation of the mechanical reaper in the 1830s
the invention of the cotton gin in the 1790s
the establishment of joint-stock companies in the 1600s
